messages.content: Build a minimal program that reads a user id, computes a greeting, and prints it using Effect.gen. Execute it at the entry point with Effect.runPromise and log the resolved string.
How do I execute a pure, synchronous Effect without going through a Promise? Show a minimal Effect.runSync example.
Write an Effect program that performs a background computation using Effect.runFork and exits the process when the resulting fiber completes. Use typed success/failure channels.
Constraints: no `any`, must use Effect.gen for sequencing, and must use Effect.runPromise at the boundary. Build a program that doubles a number, adds one, and prints it.
Show how to pair Effect.runPromiseExit with Exit.match so caller code can handle both success and failure without throwing.
Refactor this top-level code to use Effect.runPromise and preserve its typed success channel: `async function main() { const x = 1 + 1; console.log(x); }`
Build from scratch: an Effect program that forks a long-running daemon fiber with Effect.forkDaemon, joins it after a short delay, and returns its result. Use typed Effect.gen.
How do I spawn a background fiber and later await its result with Fiber.join? Show a minimal Effect v3 example.
Explain briefly how Effect.forkDaemon differs from Effect.fork, then show a typed implementation that uses forkDaemon to outlive its parent scope and is later joined via a fiber handle.

Constraints: typed error channel, no `any`, must use Effect.forkDaemon. Build a program that forks a daemon fiber that might fail with a tagged error, then awaits its Exit through Fiber.join + Effect.exit.
Add a manual interrupt capability to this forked fiber: `const fiber = yield* Effect.fork(longTask)`. Show how Fiber.interrupt cooperates with Fiber.join.
Build from scratch: an Effect program marked interruptible that sleeps in a loop, so a parent fiber can cancel it promptly via Fiber.interrupt.
What's the Effect v3 way to protect a critical region from interruption? Show Effect.uninterruptible with a minimal example.
Explain briefly how Effect.uninterruptibleMask works with a restore callback, then show a typed implementation.

Capability add-on: make this computation interruption-safe by wrapping its finalizer in Effect.uninterruptible. Assume the original is `const withLock = (eff: Effect.Effect<A>) => Effect.acquireUseRelease(acquire, () => eff, release)`.
Constraint-driven: typed, no `any`, use Effect.interruptible explicitly to mark a nested effect cancellable even when the outer region is uninterruptible.
Build from scratch: a fp-ts validator that uses `These` to accumulate warnings along with a successful value. A username below 3 chars fails; above 20 chars yields the value plus a warning.
How do I collapse a fp-ts These value into an Either while still reporting the accumulated errors? Show a minimal example.
Explain briefly how fp-ts These differs from Either, then show a typed validator that yields valid, invalid, or both.
Constraints: typed, no `any`, use fp-ts These. Build a function that accumulates warnings across two field validators and still returns the trimmed record if at least one side succeeded.
Show the fp-ts These way to transform only the success side of a Both without losing the warnings. Brief example.
Build from scratch: a typed Effect program that fails half the time, then uses Effect.exit + Exit.match to print distinct messages for success and failure.
How do I convert an Effect's failure into an Exit value so I can inspect the Cause? Minimal Effect v3 example.
Explain briefly how Exit.match branches on success vs failure vs interruption, then show a typed implementation that logs a distinct message per branch.
Targeted fix: this program throws on failure. Rewrite it to use Effect.exit + Exit.match so the caller can branch safely. `const run = Effect.runPromise(flakyTask)`.
Constraints: no `any`, typed error channel, use Effect.exit and Exit.match. Write a helper `settle` that converts Effect<A, E> to Effect<Result<A, E>> where Result is a discriminated union.
Capability add-on: extend this pipeline with Exit-based retry logic. Only retry on typed failures, not on interrupts. Base: `const task = fetchOrder(id)`.
Build from scratch: take an array of numbers and in one pipe, drop the odd ones, double the rest, and sum them using fp-ts ReadonlyArray traversals.
What's the fp-ts way to traverse an array with a short-circuiting Either? Show a minimal A.traverse example.
Explain briefly how A.filterMap differs from A.map followed by A.filter, then show a typed example extracting parsed integers from mixed strings.
Constraints: no `any`, use fp-ts ReadonlyArray + Option. Build a function that looks up each id in a map, returning the list of hits in the original order and dropping misses.
Capability add-on: extend this pipeline with a traverse step that validates each row and collects the first error. Base: `const rows: ReadonlyArray<string> = raw.split(',')`.
Show the fp-ts way to index-zip two arrays without losing the shorter-array bound, then map the pairs with a typed function.
Build from scratch: an expensive Effect that doubles its input and sleeps, then memoize it with Effect.cached so repeated access within the same fiber runs the work once.
How do I memoize the result of an Effect across multiple dependents without running it twice? Show a minimal Effect.cached example.
Explain briefly how Effect.cachedWithTTL differs from Effect.cached, then show a typed example that re-evaluates every 100 milliseconds.
Refactor this code to memoize the config fetch so parallel reads don't duplicate work: `const cfg = Effect.flatMap(fetchConfig, (c) => Effect.succeed(c.flag))`.
Constraints: typed, no `any`, use Effect.cached. Build a helper that makes any Effect idempotent within a single request scope.
Capability add-on: take this effect and give it a cached view plus a force-refresh mode. Base: `const fetchRates: Effect.Effect<number>`.
Build from scratch: an Effect program that forks two independent workers with Effect.fork, joins both with Fiber.join, and returns the sum of their results.
